questions.question_text: Is an attorney required to conduct a real estate closing in Tennessee?
questions.correct_answer: No, Tennessee is an attorney-optional state
distractors: Yes, an attorney must be present at closing
Yes, but only for properties over $300,000
Only for transactions involving a mortgage
questions.explanation: Tennessee is an attorney-optional state. Closings can be conducted by title companies, attorneys, or other closing agents. Attorney involvement is recommended but not mandatory.

questions.question_text: What is the standard practice in Tennessee to verify clear ownership of property before closing?
questions.correct_answer: A title search of public records
distractors: A verbal warranty from the seller
questions.explanation: A title search is standard practice to identify liens, encumbrances, and ownership history. It examines public records to verify the seller has clear title to convey.

questions.question_text: A buyer purchases a home in Tennessee. Which types of title insurance are commonly obtained?
distractors: Only the lender's policy is needed
Only the owner's policy is required
Title insurance is not used in Tennessee
questions.explanation: Both owner's and lender's title insurance policies are commonly used in Tennessee. The lender's policy protects the mortgage lender; the owner's policy protects the buyer's equity.

questions.question_text: A title search reveals an unresolved mechanic's lien on the property. How does this affect closing?
questions.correct_answer: The lien must be resolved before clear title
distractors: The buyer assumes the lien automatically
Title insurance covers all existing liens
The lien expires after the closing
questions.explanation: An unresolved mechanic's lien is an encumbrance on the title. It must be satisfied or resolved before the seller can convey clear title to the buyer at closing.

questions.question_text: Who typically conducts real estate closings in Tennessee when no attorney is involved?
distractors: The real estate agents for buyer and seller
questions.explanation: When an attorney is not conducting the closing, title companies or other licensed closing agents typically handle the settlement process in Tennessee.

questions.question_text: Where are deeds recorded in Tennessee?
questions.correct_answer: With the county Register of Deeds
distractors: With the Tennessee Secretary of State
questions.explanation: Deeds and other instruments affecting real property are recorded with the Register of Deeds in the county where the property is located.

questions.question_text: What type of recording statute does Tennessee follow?
questions.explanation: Tennessee is a race-notice recording state. An unrecorded instrument is void against a subsequent purchaser who records first without notice of the prior conveyance.

questions.question_text: Buyer A purchases property but does not record the deed. Buyer B later purchases the same property without knowledge of Buyer A's deed and records immediately. Who prevails in Tennessee?
questions.correct_answer: Buyer B, who recorded first without notice
distractors: Buyer A, as the first purchaser in time
The seller, since both deeds are void
questions.explanation: Under Tennessee's race-notice statute, Buyer B prevails because they recorded first AND had no notice of Buyer A's prior unrecorded deed. Both conditions must be met.

questions.question_text: What does recording a deed provide to the new owner?
distractors: Actual notice to all potential buyers
questions.explanation: Recording provides constructive notice — the legal presumption that the public has been informed of the ownership transfer. It does not guarantee clear title or provide insurance.

questions.question_text: In Tennessee's race-notice system, a subsequent buyer who knew about a prior unrecorded deed records first. Who has priority?
questions.correct_answer: The first buyer, because the second had notice
distractors: The second buyer, who recorded first
Neither, the deeds cancel each other
Whichever buyer paid the higher price
questions.explanation: Under race-notice, the subsequent buyer must both record first AND have no notice of the prior conveyance. If they had notice of the prior deed, they cannot claim priority even by recording first.

questions.question_text: Which type of deed provides the broadest protection to the buyer in Tennessee?
questions.explanation: A warranty deed provides the broadest protection because the grantor warrants title against all defects, past and present. It provides the strongest guarantees of clear title.

questions.question_text: A property owner wants to transfer interest to a family member quickly with no warranties. Which deed should be used?
questions.explanation: A quitclaim deed conveys only whatever interest the grantor has with no warranties. It is commonly used for family transfers, divorce settlements, and clearing title defects.

questions.question_text: A special warranty deed warrants title against defects arising during which time period?
questions.correct_answer: Only during the grantor's ownership period
distractors: The entire history of the property
The last 10 years before the transfer
questions.explanation: A special warranty deed warrants only against defects that arose during the grantor's period of ownership, not against issues from prior owners.

questions.question_text: A buyer discovers a title defect from 30 years ago after purchasing with a special warranty deed. Can the buyer sue the grantor for this defect?
questions.correct_answer: No, the defect predates the grantor's ownership
distractors: Yes, all title defects are warranted
Yes, if the defect was in public records
Only if the purchase price was over $200,000
questions.explanation: A special warranty deed only warrants against defects from the grantor's ownership period. A 30-year-old defect predates the grantor and would not be covered by the warranty.

questions.question_text: Which deed conveys whatever interest the grantor has without any title promises?
questions.explanation: A quitclaim deed conveys only the interest the grantor currently holds, with no warranties or guarantees of any kind about the quality of the title.

questions.question_text: What is the primary security instrument used for real estate loans in Tennessee?
questions.explanation: Tennessee primarily uses deeds of trust rather than mortgages. A deed of trust is a three-party instrument involving the borrower (trustor), lender (beneficiary), and trustee.

questions.question_text: In a Tennessee deed of trust, who holds the legal title until the loan is paid?
questions.explanation: In a deed of trust, the trustee holds legal title as security until the loan is paid. Once the loan is satisfied, the trustee reconveys title to the borrower.

questions.question_text: What type of foreclosure is typically used in Tennessee?
questions.explanation: Tennessee typically uses non-judicial foreclosure (power of sale) through the trustee. This process is faster than judicial foreclosure because it does not require court involvement.

questions.question_text: Before a foreclosure sale in Tennessee, notice must be published in a newspaper for how long?
questions.explanation: The notice of sale must be published in a newspaper for 3 consecutive weeks before the trustee's sale. There must be a minimum of 20 days from first publication to the sale date.

questions.question_text: After a foreclosure sale in Tennessee, how long is the statutory right of redemption period?
questions.explanation: Tennessee's statutory right of redemption is 2 years after foreclosure — unusually long compared to most states. However, this right can be waived in the deed of trust, which is common in modern practice.
